% Title and authors
\title{Adaptive Per-Distance Sampling for Efficient Multi-Scale Network Centrality Analysis}
\author{%
  Author Name\\
  \textit{Affiliation}\\
  \texttt{email@example.com}
}
\date{\today}

\begin{document}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
  Computing network centrality at scale is expensive. Sampling can help, but how much is enough? We present a practical model: maintain an effective sample size of $\effn = \max(k \cdot \sqrt{r}, n_{\min})$, where $r$ is network reach, to achieve ranking accuracy $\rho \geq \targetRho$. The proportional term ($k = \kProp$) handles large networks; the floor ($n_{\min} = \minEffN$) ensures reliability at low reach. The model validates on a \glaNnodes{}-node Greater London street network, achieving $\rho > \glaMinRho$ at distances from 5--20km with speedups of \glaFiveKmSpeedup{}--\glaTwentyKmSpeedup{}$\times$. The implementation is available in the open-source \cityseer{} Python library.
\end{abstract}

\textbf{Keywords:} network centrality, sampling, urban morphology, betweenness, harmonic closeness, computational efficiency
